speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Why, lords, what wrongs are these! Was ever seen

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: An emperor in Rome thus overborne,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Troubled, confronted thus, and for the extent

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Of equal justice, used in such contempt?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: My lords, you know, as know the mightful gods,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: However these disturbers of our peace

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Buzz in the people’s ears, there naught hath passed

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: But even with law against the willful sons

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Of old Andronicus. And what an if

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: His sorrows have so overwhelmed his wits?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Shall we be thus afflicted in his wreaks,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: His fits, his frenzy, and his bitterness?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: And now he writes to heaven for his redress!

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: See, here’s “to Jove,” and this “to Mercury,”

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: This “to Apollo,” this to the god of war.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Sweet scrolls to fly about the streets of Rome!

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: What’s this but libeling against the Senate

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: And blazoning our unjustice everywhere?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: A goodly humor is it not, my lords?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: As who would say, in Rome no justice were.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: But if I live, his feignèd ecstasies

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Shall be no shelter to these outrages,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: But he and his shall know that justice lives

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: In Saturninus’ health, whom, if he sleep,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: He’ll so awake as he in fury shall

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Cut off the proud’st conspirator that lives.

speaker: TAMORA
text: My gracious lord, my lovely Saturnine,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Lord of my life, commander of my thoughts,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Calm thee, and bear the faults of Titus’ age,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Th’ effects of sorrow for his valiant sons,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Whose loss hath pierced him deep and scarred his heart,

speaker: TAMORA
text: And rather comfort his distressèd plight

speaker: TAMORA
text: Than prosecute the meanest or the best

speaker: TAMORA
text:
For these contempts.
                 
                (
                Aside
                .
                )
               Why, thus it shall become

speaker: TAMORA
text: High-witted Tamora to gloze with all.

speaker: TAMORA
text: But, Titus, I have touched thee to the quick.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Thy lifeblood out, if Aaron now be wise,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Then is all safe, the anchor in the port.

speaker: TAMORA
text: How now, good fellow, wouldst thou speak with us?

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: Yea, forsooth, an your Mistresship be

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: emperial.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Empress I am, but yonder sits the Emperor.

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: ’Tis he!—God and Saint Stephen

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: give you good e’en. I have brought you a letter and

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: a couple of pigeons here.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Go, take him away, and hang him presently.

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: How much money must I have?

speaker: TAMORA
text: Come, sirrah, you must be hanged.

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: Hanged! By ’r Lady, then I have

speaker:
COUNTRY
               
              FELLOW
text: brought up a neck to a fair end.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Despiteful and intolerable wrongs!

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Shall I endure this monstrous villainy?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: I know from whence this same device proceeds.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: May this be borne?—as if his traitorous sons,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: That died by law for murder of our brother,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Have by my means been butchered wrongfully!

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Go, drag the villain hither by the hair.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Nor age nor honor shall shape privilege.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: For this proud mock, I’ll be thy slaughterman,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Sly, frantic wretch, that holp’st to make me great

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: In hope thyself should govern Rome and me.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: What news with thee, Aemilius?

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: Arm, my lords! Rome never had more cause.

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: The Goths have gathered head, and with a power

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: Of high-resolvèd men bent to the spoil,

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: They hither march amain under conduct

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: Of Lucius, son to old Andronicus,

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: Who threats, in course of this revenge, to do

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: As much as ever Coriolanus did.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Is warlike Lucius general of the Goths?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: These tidings nip me, and I hang the head

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: As flowers with frost or grass beat down with storms.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Ay, now begins our sorrows to approach.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: ’Tis he the common people love so much.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Myself hath often heard them say,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: When I have walkèd like a private man,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: That Lucius’ banishment was wrongfully,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: And they have wished that Lucius were their emperor.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Why should you fear? Is not your city strong?

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Ay, but the citizens favor Lucius

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: And will revolt from me to succor him.

speaker: TAMORA
text: King, be thy thoughts imperious like thy name.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Is the sun dimmed that gnats do fly in it?

speaker: TAMORA
text: The eagle suffers little birds to sing

speaker: TAMORA
text: And is not careful what they mean thereby,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Knowing that with the shadow of his wings

speaker: TAMORA
text: He can at pleasure stint their melody.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Even so mayst thou the giddy men of Rome.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Then cheer thy spirit, for know, thou emperor,

speaker: TAMORA
text: I will enchant the old Andronicus

speaker: TAMORA
text: With words more sweet and yet more dangerous

speaker: TAMORA
text: Than baits to fish or honey-stalks to sheep,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Whenas the one is wounded with the bait,

speaker: TAMORA
text: The other rotted with delicious feed.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: But he will not entreat his son for us.

speaker: TAMORA
text: If Tamora entreat him, then he will,

speaker: TAMORA
text: For I can smooth and fill his agèd ears

speaker: TAMORA
text: With golden promises, that were his heart

speaker: TAMORA
text: Almost impregnable, his old ears deaf,

speaker: TAMORA
text: Yet should both ear and heart obey my tongue.

speaker: TAMORA
text:
To
                 
                Aemilius
                .
                 
              Go thou before to be our ambassador.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Say that the Emperor requests a parley

speaker: TAMORA
text: Of warlike Lucius, and appoint the meeting

speaker: TAMORA
text: Even at his father’s house, the old Andronicus.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Aemilius, do this message honorably,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: And if he stand in hostage for his safety,

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Bid him demand what pledge will please him best.

speaker: AEMILIUS
text: Your bidding shall I do effectually.

speaker: TAMORA
text: Now will I to that old Andronicus

speaker: TAMORA
text: And temper him with all the art I have

speaker: TAMORA
text: To pluck proud Lucius from the warlike Goths.

speaker: TAMORA
text: And now, sweet emperor, be blithe again,

speaker: TAMORA
text: And bury all thy fear in my devices.

speaker: SATURNINUS
text: Then go successantly, and plead to him.
